questions.questionText: Hyperplasia is defined as an increase in the:
questions.optionA: Conversion of one adult cell type to another
questions.optionC: Number of cells in an organ or tissue
questions.explanation: Hyperplasia refers to an increased number of cells (e.g., hormonal hyperplasia in pregnancy).

questions.questionText: Barrett's esophagus is an example of which type of cellular adaptation?
questions.explanation: Squamous epithelium of the esophagus changes to columnar epithelium due to chronic acid reflux.

questions.questionText: Coagulative necrosis is classically seen in all ischemic infarctions EXCEPT in the:
questions.explanation: Ischemic injury in the brain leads to liquefactive necrosis, not coagulative.

questions.questionText: Caseous necrosis is most characteristic of which infectious disease?
questions.explanation: Caseous ('cheese-like') necrosis is the hallmark of tuberculous granulomas.

questions.questionText: The earliest ultrastructural change visible in reversible cell injury is:
questions.explanation: Mitochondrial swelling and ER dilation (cellular swelling) occur early due to ATP depletion.

questions.questionText: Which of the following describes nuclear fragmentation into small dust-like particles during cell death?
questions.explanation: Karyorrhexis is the destructive fragmentation of the nucleus of a dying cell.

questions.questionText: Fat necrosis is most commonly associated with severe inflammatory disease of the:
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipases release fatty acids which combine with calcium (saponification).

questions.questionText: Lipofuscin pigment is best described as a sign of:
questions.optionA: Free radical injury and 'wear and tear'
questions.explanation: Lipofuscin represents complexes of lipid and protein from free-radical catalyzed peroxidation.

questions.optionC: Dead or degenerating tissues with normal serum calcium
questions.explanation: Dystrophic calcification occurs locally in abnormal tissue, independently of absolute calcium levels.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is an anti-apoptotic gene?
questions.explanation: Bcl-2 is the prototype anti-apoptotic protein that prevents cytochrome c release.

questions.questionText: In Cell Injury and Adaptation, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for Cell Injury and Adaptation. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Cell Injury and Adaptation.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Cell Injury and Adaptation scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Cell Injury and Adaptation.
